sentences.text: They did not wear a uniform.
They had to wear a yellow arm band that you had to wear on top of your jacket.
Or, if you did not wear a jacket, on your shirt sleeves.
You could not go around without the yellow armband.
That was-- that was the-- that was a must.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember one Purim.
That's a holiday when [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's permitted in our traditions to drink a little more.
And we had about 50 boys coming to our house, and they drank a little.
Nobody became drunk, but they were somewhat high.
The first thing these these boys did was they ripped off, one by one, the yellow armband.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I recall this story always because there's a Talmudic teaching that [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
The wine goes in, and your-- the essence of your inner self comes to the surface.
And I always mention this, that we are, first of all, a free nation.
We were always free.
Even if people enslaved us, there was no way that they could take our freedom, our emotional freedom, and the state of mind.
Our state of mind was always that of a free person.
So here's a boy who is high, who is at ease.
Instead of like what other people would do when they are high-- they would use foul language.
They would do all types of crazy things.
The first thing he does is he rips off the yellow armband, because he is a Jewish boy, and he's a free boy.
I also remember that as they left our home, they were in a good mood, and they did not want to put the yellow armband back on their sleeves.
But my mother stood at the door, and she would not let out any boy without it.
And she even sewed it on to make sure that it's on.
I remember this also well.
I remember it was late afternoon when they came to our city.
And of course, communication in those days was not like today.
And today, we would know if someone was coming from radio, or television, from papers.
It was a total surprise.
We had a very broad Boulevard near where I lived.
And I see them marching down.
They came always, constantly parading.
And it was a form of rabble-rousing, I believe.
And they would use their drums and their music, and their bands, and march.
And people on both sides would stand and cheer them on, and clap their hands, and throw flowers at them, and with tremendous joy, receive them.
And I remember going that evening to the synagogue, to the shul.
People were terribly upset.
Many said, this is our end.
Many-- many people said that.
This is our end.
I would like to bring in a small story, if I may, because I think it's very relevant.
It happened about a year before that.
We had a Jew who came to us from Poland, who escaped from one of the concentration camps.
He did not tell us from where.
I think he was very afraid to speak openly.
And he sits down.
And I remember being in the room also, standing next to my father.
My mother is at the opposite end of this table.
And he says in Yiddish to my father, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
Rabbi, you know they are making soap out of people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father reacted in a certain shocking manner.
And my mother said, you know, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
It cannot be.
Now, here is a man who comes from one of the concentration camps in Poland.
He comes to a rabbi, and we did not believe him.
To this day, I feel bad for this man that he was very upset.
That we doubted this.
But the Germans were such masters of deception, that we who were in it and see a man running away from it, we still would not believe it.
I think that this is significant.
I really was not completely aware of that.
I only knew there was a big war, and we are on the other side.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I knew that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they-- over in a lot of Europe.
But I did not to look at the Germans, let's say, as the fathers of Nazis and war with the Nazis.
We had them right home.
To us, the Nazis were the Hungarians.
As a matter of fact, in some respects, they were more Nazis than the Germans.
And even their leader, [INAUDIBLE] Hitler, came from Austria.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I knew there was a struggle for our survival, for our existence.
But I did not really realize the full detail.
It's important to remember that we did not have television, we did not have radio, and the press was very limited.
We just knew what we heard.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There wasn't an awareness, like today.
I know from history the exact scope of Nazism.
Soon the Jewish stores had to close.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Hungarian Nazis themselves became more verbal, more loud, more dominant, more eminent.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every day, they had new regulations.
New rules, new regulations.
The first, the closing of the Jewish stores.
Second, every Jew had to wear a yellow star.
Children also.
Men, women, children.
You had to go on the street, you wore a yellow star.
Then they started to build a ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which we were in the center of the Jewish neighborhood, so we were in it.
And they built a concrete wall around our neighborhood.
A tremendous effort.
I saw them working day and night building this wall.
They would ask families who lived further away to move into the ghetto, and families who lived in the ghetto to share their apartments with other families.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We really began to lose our freedom.
The constant flow of people that we were used in my house stopped.
There was no more traffic, no more in or out.
And each day became more repressive than the day before.

We-- One event I will always remember from those days, from those weeks before the deportation, was there was-- they brought in the Jews from the neighboring villages, and they put them-- locked them up in camps in our city.
And there was a woman there, a well known lady from a very special family.
And she was about to give birth.
And my dad arranged to get a bed, a special type of bed upon which a woman gives birth.
There was no place else to put it in but in the synagogue itself, in our shul.
And my father asked them.
They still had some respect for a rabbi there, in a big city.
And the authorities let this lady out for the duration of giving birth.
And she gave birth to a baby in front of the Holy Ark in our synagogue.
And this baby is, thank God, alive today.
And he is, in fact, a Hasidic rabbi in Williamsburg.
He lives his call today, that [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was at his bris, at his circumcision, eight days later, which was also in the shul.
And they gave permission to have this.
And at that time, in our city was also locked up a rabbi who became very prominent now, was the rabbi of a village near us called [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he came from a Hasidic dynasty, the Teitelbaum family.
And my father asked they should let him out for a few hours, which they did.
And he had the honor of being the sandek, the one who holds the baby during the circumcision.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my father, as always, took me to be part of this.
We took-- we got together 10 people, a minion.
And the bris was there.
Afterwards, everybody cried bitterly.
And everybody made a statement that was-- really impressed me, and-- and-- impressed me and depressed me.
They said, well now his fate is sealed.
That was the statement.
Instead of-- usually at the bris, there is a joyous environment.
This was a very sad moment.
His fate is sealed.
My father cried bitterly.
Also, this rabbi of [NON-ENGLISH], who today is known as the rabbi of Satmar, cried bitterly.
When my dad and him met later in the United States, he asked my father.
He said, [NON-ENGLISH], Rabbi of [NON-ENGLISH], you cried so at this bris.
Did you know what's happening to us?
Did you know where they are taking us?
And my father told him, no.
I didn't know where they are taking us, but I know it's not a good place.
The synagogue was outside of the ghetto?
Inside the ghetto.
Yes.
I-- we lived on a street called [NON-ENGLISH].
In fact, the street was-- my house number is 28.
And some of my family-- I didn't have a chance to ever visit there again.
Some of my family visited there.
My mother visited, and she said the house is there.
In fact, she went into the kitchen.
There's a family living in that apartment.
And she made some renovations in the kitchen.
And she said, the renovations are still the way it was in those days.
No.
We already stopped schooling.
Basically, there was no schooling, even before the Germans arrived.
It was already not safe.
The atmosphere was already poisoned.
There was too much hooliganism.
There was too much kidnapping on the street.
And it was just not safe.
And still, you know, in the context of all this, the deception was great.
It's an unbelievable-- they-- I think the Germans were masters of deception.
When this baby was born, he had no diapers.
My mother asked me, I should rip off my yellow star, and take off my yarmulke, and jump the fence, and go and buy diapers in a store, which I did.
I ran, like breathless.
I didn't question the safety of it, because my parents sent me.
Of course, I didn't realize exactly the danger.
To this day, I want you to know-- to this day, my mother says, how did we do this?
To take such a risk?
If they catch me, they would shoot me on the spot.
But the deception was unbelievable.
And we really did not realize the extent of the danger that we are in.
Who guarded the ghetto?
They had the gens d'armes.
That was that special police who were very vicious.
We had some food.
There were some stores that were open.
The ghetto itself did not last that long.
It wasn't like ghettos in Poland that lasted an extensive period of time.
The whole ghetto was a few short weeks before deportation time.
In Hungary, the Germans had already a lot of experience, and they worked very fast.
No.
I-- nobody tried to intervene.
Nobody tried to help.
Hungarians were very anti-Semitic, and they supported this with great love and zest.
And when they deported us on the open trucks, we saw people all around the streets happy, joyful, and smiling.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Hungarians were very glad to come and take over the Jewish apartments.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was absolutely no sympathy or help.
And we lived in an-- in an open society, and we were very friendly with them.
We went to their birthdays.
They came to ours.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this was very blatant.
I imagine the most blatant aspect of this was that these best of friends deserted you in the moment of need.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They just-- they supported the enemy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then you develop the feeling that remains to this day, I imagine, that you don't trust these people.
Yes.
We had a Judenrat.
There was a Jewish council.
And I remember that they were the ones who came one night, about 12 midnight.
We lived in an apartment house.
And the apartment house had an inner court, U-shaped.
And they came and made a big announcement in the middle of the night that everybody has to pack and get ready to leave at 6:00 AM.
No.
No.
There was no Jewish police.
The ghetto barracks was very short lived.
A few short weeks.
A few short weeks.
It was a horrible night.
There was bitter crying and weeping.
In our own apartment house, there were a few suicides.
People would jump from the upper floors into the yard.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my parents were crying all night.
My mother went and prepared the bath.
She gave a bath to all the children [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and packed certain belongings.
But it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the feeling was like someone who is sentenced to die.
It was extremely traumatic, unusually traumatic.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the morning, about 5 o'clock I was up already.
It was spring, summer, and the sun came up early.
And the neighbors asked me.
They called me [INAUDIBLE], come on up.
Come on up.
And everybody stuffed me this money.
I was a little bit too naive to realize that the money had no value at that time.
But I remember receiving the equivalent of thousands of dollars in Hungarian currency.
And I ran with a smile to my dad.
See how much money we have?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, this whole money lasted just a few hours.
Because when we left, and they assembled us at the Jewish Community Center over there, everybody was stripped, and everybody had to open his pocket inside out.
And many people were asked to get undressed.
That was the first time I saw that.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's the reason-- if I may digress for a second-- when they showed in people in nudity, and there were some religious groups who criticized that.
I was there, and I saw it.
And when these people would ask me, I was like, transported back to those days.
And I said, you know, if this was beautiful in the eyes of God, if God wants-- I get so emotional with this-- that his nation should look like this, then so be it.
That this is how we're going to show it.
This was beautiful in God's eyes, we'll show it like this.
And we show it like this.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's how it was.
They asked everyone to get undressed and to throw all their belongings out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they took away every valuable, money and jewelry [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and toys.
You don't need it where you are going.
My father has a [INAUDIBLE] and a [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think we had one or two prayer books, and that's it.
Not everything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I believe that we retained the prayer book.
no.
No.
We-- we got dressed in our clothing.
They did not take our clothing away.
They just took away all the valuables, money, and jewelry, and toys from children.
But then they put us on trucks, open trucks.
And they took us to the edge of the city, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where they told us that we will stay there for a while.
It was a brick factory the Hungarians called the [NON-ENGLISH].
A brick factory in an open field, with some cover and straw.
And that was our home for about two weeks.
Yes, that neighborhood.
That neighborhood.
That neighborhood was a separate neighborhood at the outskirt of [NON-ENGLISH].
And near there was also the second railway station from where we got eventually deported, not from the main railroad station.
People from other cities were brought to [NON-ENGLISH].
But by the time they took us there, all these other people were already deported.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The first segment of Jews who were deported from Hungary were from the small towns.
There was a certain gradualness about this.
The more anti-Semitic the city, the-- the faster they were deported.
So in the small towns, where people were less civilized, they would be deported faster.
That's why they brought in all the Jews from the neighboring villages to our town, and they would be deported.
By the time the ghetto in [NON-ENGLISH] was broken up, all those people were deported.
And we stayed in this place to wait transport, again, guarded by the Hungarians.
All this was done by Hungarians.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they-- they-- they loved this.
They enjoy this.
That's why when I meet these people, and they tell me, well, it was done on command of the Germans, I tell them, I didn't see too many Germans.
I remember Hungarians.
And they enjoyed this.
They did it with a zest and a zeal.
They were very exceptionally cruel, the Hungarians.
They asked everyone to line up three times a day against the wall.
They said about face, and a machine gun behind us.
We were sure they would shoot us on the spot.
And I remember clinging to my father's hand.
And my father would recite the prayer [INAUDIBLE].
This went on three times a day.
As you pray three times a day, we did this three times a day.
This was a way of torturing us.
And they also took out the people from the mental institution that was in [NON-ENGLISH], the Jews.
And they kept them locked up in a chicken coop out there.
The chicken coop was about three feet high.
So these people could only sit.
They could not stand up or stretch out.
It was a horrible torture for all these people.
And-- and they were left there in their dirt, and just very unsanitary.
And this was in a public display.
All along you were together with your family?
Yes.
All along we were together.
And then gradually, they with deport people from the city.
And we were the-- the very last group to be deported.
Yes.
No.
No.
They didn't.
They didn't.
I don't believe they assumed that there was anything hidden.
It was just too fast.
This-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the moment that the Germans entered Hungary to the time that they deported us was an extremely short period of time.
It-- it like happened overnight.
And we were so naive.
And the Germans did such a great job of deception.
We didn't believe that this would actually happen to us.
We lived in a very-- relatively liberal society.
We-- we couldn't believe it.
OK.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If I need to refer to my note, because I want to mention the name of a city that-- yes.
I have it here.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One of the reasons we did not believe that this is-- that we are going to a death camp, because for years now, for at least two years, when they would take a Jewish boy into the [INAUDIBLE], or the work brigade, or they would even arrest a Jew in the street sometimes and send him away.
We-- my father especially, the rabbi of the city, would get from him a postcard.
This postcard was marked Valdeza.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it says, dear rabbi, we are being treated well.
We are working.
And best regards, signed such and such.
[NON-ENGLISH] This Valdeza-- of course we did not realize-- was a fictitious entity somewhere in hell, I would say.
It just did not exist.
It was a deception.
And everyone who was about to be killed was asked to write this postcard.
And they stamp marked it Valdeza.
We thought there is a Valdeza.
But there was no Valdeza.
And we really figured that everyone is being taken to Valdeza.
And the rumor was that because the gentiles had this propaganda there that the Jews are lazy, they are only into their professions, they should do some physical work.
This is really how we bought the story.
They want us to do physical work.
They don't like that we should be doctors or lawyers.
We should do physical work.
And here in Valdeza, it's like a re-education village.
And they will teach us to do productive, physical labor.
And we were certain that everyone's going to Valdeza.
And we knew that one of these days, the war is going to come to an end.
And so we will survive Valdeza.
It's a question of a year or a few months.
We will survive it.
And everybody thought that we were going to Valdeza.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In fact, this customer once visited my grandpa in Vienna.
And my grandfather told him, you know, I assume that my family is in Valdeza.
So the customer said, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
He used that expression to my grandfather.
That's a deception.
There's no Valdeza.
